name: Ravana
description: Ravana, the ten-headed demon king of Lanka, is a prominent figure in Hindu mythology, primarily known for his role as the antagonist in the epic Ramayana. Born to the sage Vishrava and the demon princess Kaikesi, Ravana inherited both Brahminical wisdom and Rakshasa strength. Through intense asceticism, he pleased Lord Brahma and secured a boon of invincibility against gods, demons, and animals, excluding humans. Arrogant and powerful, Ravana terrorized the world, conquering kingdoms and challenging the gods. He abducted Sita, the wife of Lord Rama, triggering a fierce war between Rama's army and Ravana's forces in Lanka. Despite his immense power and knowledge of scriptures, Ravana's pride and lust ultimately led to his downfall at the hands of Rama, an avatar of Vishnu. His ten heads symbolized his mastery over the four Vedas and six Shastras, as well as his ten different emotions. Ravana is often depicted as a complex character, possessing both admirable qualities like his devotion to Shiva and his mastery of various arts, and destructive traits like his arrogance and cruelty. His defeat signifies the triumph of good over evil and the importance of dharma.
subtitle: The Ten-Headed King of Lanka
achievements: Obtained boon of invincibility from Brahma
Mastered the four Vedas and six Shastras
Built the golden city of Lanka
Commanded a powerful army of Rakshasas

keyMyths.title: Ravana's Boon
keyMyths.description: Ravana performed severe penance to please Brahma. He requested immortality, but Brahma granted him invincibility against gods, demons, and animals, excluding humans, leading to his arrogance.

keyMyths.title: Abduction of Sita
keyMyths.description: Driven by revenge and lust, Ravana abducted Sita, the wife of Rama, and imprisoned her in Ashok Vatika in Lanka. This act initiated the epic war between Rama and Ravana.

keyMyths.title: The Battle of Lanka
keyMyths.description: The war between Rama's army and Ravana's forces was a clash of divine power and demonic strength. Ultimately, Rama defeated Ravana, restoring dharma and rescuing Sita.

sources.author: Valmiki
sources.description: The primary source for the story of Ravana and his conflict with Rama.
sources.text: The primary source for the story of Ravana and his conflict with Rama.

sources.author: Tulsidas
sources.description: A popular retelling of the Ramayana, offering a different perspective on Ravana.
sources.text: A popular retelling of the Ramayana, offering a different perspective on Ravana.

associations.name: Ten Heads
associations.significance: Symbolizes his vast knowledge of the Vedas and Shastras, as well as his immense power and ego.

associations.name: Chandrahas
associations.significance: A celestial sword granted by Lord Shiva, representing Ravana's prowess in battle and his divine favor.

associations.name: Lanka
associations.significance: His golden city, a symbol of his wealth, power, and architectural genius.

associations.name: Veena
associations.significance: Represents his musical talents and refined nature, contrasting with his demonic persona.

associations.name: Atma Linga
associations.significance: The Shiva Linga he attempted to carry to Lanka, symbolizing his devotion and desire for immortality.

associations.name: Arrogance
associations.significance: Represents the destructive nature of unchecked power and pride, leading to his downfall.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both figures possess immense strength and bear significant burdens, Atlas holding the sky and Ravana ruling Lanka.
Both characters are driven by ambition and a thirst for power, ultimately leading to their tragic demise.
Both represent chaos and opposition to the established order, challenging the reign of the gods.

worshipPractices.name: Dasara Celebrations
worshipPractices.description: In some regions, particularly in Sri Lanka and certain parts of India, Ravana is venerated for his knowledge and devotion to Shiva. Effigies of Ravana are burned during Dasara, symbolizing the triumph of good over evil, but some communities also acknowledge his positive attributes.

cultural.modernLegacy: Ravana remains a complex and controversial figure in modern Hindu society. He is often portrayed as a symbol of evil, but some view him as a learned scholar and a powerful warrior who challenged the established order. Interpretations vary depending on cultural and regional perspectives.

extendedContent.title: Early Life and Asceticism
extendedContent.content: Born to the sage Vishrava and the demon princess Kaikesi, Ravana's lineage blended Brahminical wisdom and Rakshasa strength. His early life was marked by intense asceticism and devotion to Lord Brahma. Driven by a desire for power and immortality, Ravana performed severe penances, enduring extreme conditions and unwavering focus. Pleased by his dedication, Brahma granted him a boon of invincibility against gods, demons, and animals, inadvertently excluding humans, which would later be the cause of his demise. This boon, coupled with his inherent strength and intellect, fueled his ambition and laid the foundation for his future conquests. His upbringing instilled in him both a deep understanding of religious texts and a ruthless determination to achieve his goals, setting the stage for his rise to power as the king of Lanka.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Quests and Conquests
extendedContent.content: Ravana's quest for power led him on numerous conquests across the three worlds. He defeated and subjugated gods, demons, and kings, amassing immense wealth and establishing his dominion over Lanka. His most notable conquest was his defeat of Kubera, the god of wealth, from whom he seized the golden city of Lanka and the Pushpaka Vimana, a flying chariot. He challenged and humbled powerful deities, including Indra, the king of the gods. Ravana's ambition knew no bounds, and he sought to establish his supremacy over all creation. His military campaigns were characterized by brutality and cunning, solidifying his reputation as a formidable warrior and a ruthless ruler. These quests demonstrated his unparalleled strength and strategic brilliance, but also highlighted his arrogance and disregard for dharma.

extendedContent.title: Legacy and Interpretations
extendedContent.content: Ravana's legacy is complex and multifaceted. While he is primarily known as the antagonist in the Ramayana, his character has been subject to diverse interpretations over time. He is often portrayed as a symbol of evil, representing the destructive forces of ego and unchecked ambition. However, some interpretations also acknowledge his positive qualities, such as his profound knowledge of the Vedas, his mastery of music, and his unwavering devotion to Lord Shiva. In certain regions, Ravana is even venerated as a wise and powerful king. His story serves as a cautionary tale about the dangers of pride and the importance of dharma, but also raises questions about the nature of good and evil and the complexities of human character. His enduring presence in Hindu mythology ensures that he will continue to be a subject of debate and interpretation for generations to come.

shortDescription: Ravana, the ten-headed demon king of Lanka, is a powerful figure in Hindu mythology, known as the antagonist in the Ramayana, symbolizing ego and ambition.
